Q1: I organized my study routine around listening to lectures and audio materials, usually during commutes or while doing other tasks. Being 28 and juggling responsibilities, I had to be strategic about my time. I'd typically block out a few hours in the evenings, maybe three or four nights a week, and then longer sessions on weekends. I found that listening to recorded lectures multiple times helped things stick better for me than just reading.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied depending on what was due and what else was happening in my life. Some weeks I'd be consistent with evening study sessions, but when deadlines piled up or work got intense, everything shifted to crisis mode. I tried to maintain some regularity because the anxiety of falling behind was worse than the effort of keeping up, but honestly, my attendance being around 50% shows that consistency wasn't my strong suit.
Q3: Usually it was either checking for new announcements, seeing if grades were posted, or when I needed to submit something. I'd also log in when I felt that familiar anxiety creeping up—like I needed to reassure myself I wasn't missing anything critical. Sometimes I'd just browse the discussion forums to see what other students were talking about, which helped me feel connected even when I was struggling to keep up.
Q4: Yeah, after a few weeks I got into a rhythm where I'd check the LMS first thing in the morning with my coffee, almost like checking email. The discussion posts became something I actually looked forward to because I'm an auditory learner and reading other people's perspectives was like hearing different voices explain the material. Once I set up notifications on my phone, it became automatic to check in regularly.
Q5: The video lectures were absolutely essential for me—I watched nearly 200 minutes worth because hearing explanations rather than just reading them made everything click. The discussion forums were also valuable because engaging with peers gave me different angles on the material. I posted quite a bit and interacted with classmates because talking through concepts, even in writing, helped solidify my understanding.
Q6: I'll be honest, I often skimmed or delayed the dense reading materials, especially PDFs with walls of text. My brain just doesn't absorb written content as efficiently as audio or discussion-based learning. Some of the supplementary resources felt redundant after watching the videos, so I'd skip them unless I was really struggling with a concept.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when it directly connected to what I'd be tested on or when it genuinely helped me understand something I was confused about. I also valued activities that let me engage with other people—whether through discussions or peer feedback. If something felt like busywork or wasn't clearly tied to my success in the course, I'd deprioritize it, especially when my anxiety was already high.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a luxury I couldn't always afford. When I was stressed and barely keeping up with required work, optional stuff went straight to the bottom of the list. But when I had breathing room and something optional looked interesting or relevant, I'd dive in. The pressure was just different—required activities came with consequences, optional ones felt more like opportunities I might miss.
Q9: Absolutely. I downloaded a lot of audio files and listened to them on my phone during commutes and while exercising. I also used external YouTube videos and podcasts that covered similar topics because sometimes hearing a different explanation helped. My notes were all in a physical notebook that I'd review constantly, and none of that showed up in any system.
Q10: There were many times I'd download a lecture, listen to it three or four times over several days, and process it deeply—but the LMS would only show one download or one view. I also had study sessions with a friend where we'd discuss course material for hours, working through problems together. That collaborative learning was some of my most effective studying, but it was completely invisible online.
Q11: All the time. I'd access a video lecture once to download it, then replay it offline multiple times over the following week. Same with readings—I'd save them to my tablet and annotate them offline. The LMS would show minimal activity, but I was actually engaging with that material repeatedly. It was more convenient and helped me manage my data usage too.
Q12: There were definitely rough patches, especially mid-semester when my anxiety spiked and everything felt overwhelming. Work responsibilities would pile up, and I'd feel paralyzed trying to decide what to prioritize. The CBT sessions I attended helped me develop coping strategies, but there were still weeks where I just couldn't bring myself to engage meaningfully. Boredom also hit when material felt repetitive or when I was too stressed to focus.
Q13: Definitely. When I was exhausted from work or dealing with competing deadlines, I'd log into the LMS but just kind of stare at the screen without really processing anything. Confusion about certain topics made me avoid those modules entirely for a while, which only increased my anxiety. My 52% attendance reflects those periods when life just got in the way of showing up consistently.
Q14: More often than I'd like to admit. Sometimes I'd have a video playing but my mind would be elsewhere, worrying about other things or just feeling too burned out to concentrate. I'd scroll through discussion posts without really reading them, just to feel like I was doing something. Those sessions looked like engagement in the data but were pretty hollow in terms of actual learning.
Q15: I definitely procrastinated on assignments that felt overwhelming or ambiguous. When instructions weren't clear or a task seemed too big, my anxiety would spike and I'd avoid it until the pressure of the deadline forced action. I knew it was counterproductive—the CBT work helped me see that pattern—but breaking it was harder than recognizing it. Sometimes I'd do easier tasks first just to feel productive, even when harder ones were more urgent.
Q16: Deadlines were both my enemy and my motivator. I'd often have bursts of intense LMS activity in the 24-48 hours before something was due, which probably shows in my login patterns. The structure of having deadlines helped me prioritize, but it also meant I was often working under pressure rather than spreading effort evenly. My response time averaging around 16 hours reflects that last-minute approach.
Q17: Feedback definitely affected my engagement, though sometimes not immediately. Positive feedback boosted my confidence and made me more likely to engage with similar activities. Critical feedback could either motivate me to work harder or, if I was already anxious, make me want to avoid that type of work for a while. I appreciated detailed feedback most because it gave me something concrete to work with.
Q18: The course structure with its multiple modules and clear deadlines helped me know what to focus on, even if I didn't always follow through consistently. Having 17 online courses worth of experience meant I knew how to navigate these systems, but this particular course's pacing sometimes felt rushed. The discussion requirements actually helped my engagement because they forced me to interact with peers, which I found valuable.
Q19: The video content being available for download made engagement much easier for my learning style. What made it harder was when important information was buried in long text documents or when the platform was clunky to navigate. Having clear module structures helped, but sometimes the sheer volume of resources was overwhelming—I didn't always know what was essential versus supplementary.
Q20: I think that could reflect people like me who are processing and exploring but struggling to produce finished work. High anxiety can make you check and recheck things obsessively without actually completing anything. It could also be students who are genuinely trying to understand but hitting walls, or people who are engaged with the community aspect but struggling with individual assignments.
Q21: That resonates with me somewhat. I performed reasonably well despite inconsistent attendance because I studied effectively offline and found ways to learn that didn't require constant LMS presence. Some students just learn efficiently and don't need to spend hours on the platform. It's also possible they have strong foundational knowledge or learn well from minimal, focused interactions with the material.
Q22: Course logs can accurately show patterns like when you're most active, how often you engage with different types of content, and whether you're meeting deadlines. But they completely miss the quality of engagement, offline studying, collaborative learning with peers outside the system, and the emotional state behind the activity. A stressed student frantically clicking around looks the same as an engaged student methodically working through material.
Q23: Being engaged means genuinely trying to understand and apply the material, not just going through the motions. It's about feeling connected to the content and the learning community, asking questions when confused, and caring about your own growth. For me, engagement includes the mental wrestling with concepts that happens away from any screen—in the shower, on walks, in conversations with friends.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show my anxiety levels fluctuating throughout the semester, or how the CBT sessions helped me develop better coping strategies. It wouldn't capture my offline listening sessions, my notebook full of handwritten notes, or the conversations with classmates that deepened my understanding. Most importantly, it wouldn't show that despite feeling bored and anxious much of the time, I was genuinely trying to climb my way through this course and improve myself.
